article_title: Proxmox Backup Server is the wrong default for small home labs
core_thesis: For small home labs with only a few VMs and containers, the built-in Proxmox backup system is often more appropriate than Proxmox Backup Server (PBS). PBS adds significant overhead—another system to maintain, update, and troubleshoot—without proportional benefits for small setups. The author argues that reliability comes from simplicity and ease of verification, not from feature lists, and that many homelabbers default to PBS without honestly assessing their actual needs.
detailed_summary: The article argues that while Proxmox Backup Server (PBS) is a powerful tool with deduplication, advanced retention, encryption, and centralized management, it is overkill for small home labs. The author describes their own experience: they initially assumed PBS was the correct choice because of its name and integration with Proxmox VE, but found that it added unnecessary complexity. Instead, they switched to using Proxmox's built-in backup jobs, which cover the basics—scheduled backups of VMs/containers to a storage target, with enough restore points for common failures like accidental deletions or bad updates. The key improvement was focusing on the restore path: backups that are easy to see, rotate, and test. Simpler jobs made verification routine and reduced the number of assumptions (e.g., another server being healthy, a datastore mounted correctly). The author acknowledges PBS's genuine advantages: chunk-level deduplication saves significant space when many similar VMs are backed up frequently, and retention policies are more elegant. However, for a small lab with few VMs, deduplication isn't transformative, and the overhead of maintaining a separate PBS server outweighs the benefits. The article concludes that the right backup system is the one you'll actually maintain, not the one with the strongest feature list. The comments strongly disagree with the author, pointing out that PBS can run on the same host, provides incremental backups that are much faster, enables disaster recovery independent of the host, and offers deduplication factors of 13x or more. Commenters also note that PBS is valuable for learning enterprise tools and that the author's concerns about complexity are overstated with automatic updates and simple configurations.

entities.name: Jeff Butts
entities.role: Author of the article, an IT professional with decades of experience in system administration and consulting.

entities.name: Proxmox Backup Server (PBS)
entities.role: A dedicated backup solution for Proxmox VE, offering deduplication, encryption, and centralized management.

entities.name: Proxmox VE
entities.role: The virtualization platform that PBS is designed to back up.

key_data_points: The author states that PBS adds another system to build, maintain, update, monitor, and understand, which increases complexity in a small home lab. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: complexity is a real cost; simpler setups are easier to maintain long-term.)

Built-in Proxmox backups can handle scheduled backups of VMs and containers, with enough restore points for common failures like accidental deletions or bad updates. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: this is sufficient for many small labs and avoids extra overhead.)

The author emphasizes that the restore path is more important than the backup engine: backups that are never tested are 'decorative objects.' (🟢 Relevant to homelab: testing restores is critical; a simple system makes testing more likely.)

PBS offers chunk-level deduplication, which can save significant storage when many similar VMs are backed up frequently. Commenters report deduplication factors of 13x or more. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: if you have many VMs, PBS can drastically reduce storage usage.)

PBS enables incremental backups that only write changed chunks, making daily full backups of a dozen VMs take only a few minutes. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: this is a major performance advantage over full backups each time.)

PBS allows disaster recovery independent of the host: if the Proxmox host dies, you can restore VMs to a new host from PBS. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: this is a key benefit for resilience that built-in backups don't provide.)

Commenters note that PBS can run on the same Proxmox host with local storage, minimizing hardware overhead. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: this addresses the author's concern about needing a separate machine.)
One commenter uses Backrest (a backup tool) to ship VFS snapshots to Backblaze, finding it simpler than PBS. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: alternative tools exist; evaluate based on your workflow.)
conclusions: For small home labs with few VMs, the built-in Proxmox backup system is often simpler and more reliable than PBS because it reduces maintenance overhead and makes verification easier. The author's experience shows that a boring, predictable backup plan is more likely to be maintained and tested.

PBS is genuinely valuable for larger labs with multiple nodes, many VMs, or strict retention needs, where its deduplication and centralized management reduce chaos. Commenters strongly advocate for PBS, citing incremental backups, disaster recovery independence, and minimal overhead when run on the same host.

The article's core lesson—that the most capable tool is not always the most appropriate—is valid, but the comments reveal that PBS's overhead is often overstated. For a homelabber willing to invest a little setup time, PBS can be a set-and-forget solution that provides significant benefits even in small setups.

The choice between PBS and built-in backups depends on specific needs: if you value incremental backups, deduplication, and host-independent recovery, PBS is worth the extra setup. If you prefer minimal complexity and have few VMs, built-in backups may suffice.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your homelab interests, as it compares two backup strategies for Proxmox environments. As a programmer and homelabber, you can take away the importance of matching tool complexity to actual needs—a lesson that applies to many DevOps decisions. The comments provide practical counterpoints: PBS can be lightweight if run on the same host, and its incremental backups and deduplication are powerful for even small labs. You should evaluate your own VM count, storage constraints, and tolerance for maintenance. If you have more than a few VMs or want host-independent recovery, PBS is likely worth the initial setup. If you prefer minimal overhead, built-in backups with a simple script to copy snapshots off-site may be sufficient. The article also reinforces the value of testing restores, which is a best practice for any backup strategy.
extra_advice: Consider running PBS as a container or lightweight VM on your existing Proxmox host with local storage—this eliminates the need for a separate machine while still gaining incremental backups and deduplication. Test both approaches: set up PBS for one VM and compare storage usage and restore speed against built-in backups. Also, automate backup verification with a simple script that restores a VM to a temporary location and checks its integrity. This hands-on comparison will give you concrete data for your specific lab size.
